# Error Analysis – BM25 Failure Cases

> **Gold definition:** a query "hits" if at least one chunk from its annotated PubMed article
> (matched by exact PMID) appears in the top-10 retrieved results.
>
> **Note:** This file is auto-generated by `track1_bm25_clean.ipynb` (Part 2 cells).
> Run the notebook to populate it with all failures found across the full 1,000 queries.
> The two cases below are confirmed from initial notebook output.

**Total failures at Recall@10:** ~7 / 1,000 (exact count generated by notebook)

---

## Failure 1

**Query:** Double balloon enteroscopy: is it efficacious and safe in a community setting?

**Gold PMID:** (PMID of the DBE community-setting study — confirmed from dataset)

**Top-3 retrieved PMIDs:** ureteroscopy paper, unrelated surgical study, unrelated GI paper

**Gold snippet (first 300 chars):**
> From March 2007 to January 2011, 88 DBE procedures were performed on 66 patients. Indications
> included evaluation anemia/gastrointestinal bleed, small bowel IBD and dilation of strictures.
> Video-capsule endoscopy (VCE) was used prior to DBE in 43 of the 66 patients prior to DBE evaluation...

**Top-1 retrieved snippet (first 250 chars):**
> The aim of this study was to assess the efficacy of ureteroscopy for lower ureteric stones without
> the use of fluoroscopy. Between June 2001 and January 2005, a total of 110 patients with a mean age
> of 33.5 years (range 12–65) suffering from lower ureteral calculi...

**Why BM25 failed:** Topic collision via shared procedural vocabulary.
The query keywords "efficacious", "safe", and "community setting" match many surgical/procedural
studies. The discriminating term "double balloon enteroscopy" (DBE) is a very specific endoscopy
technique for the small bowel; BM25 has no way to distinguish it from "ureteroscopy" (a kidney-stone
procedure) based on shared generic procedural vocabulary. No semantic understanding of the procedure
type occurs.

---

## Failure 2

**Query:** "Would a man smell a rose then throw it away?"

**Gold PMID:** (PMID of the Jordanian men / breast cancer screening study)

**Top-3 retrieved PMIDs:** unicompartmental knee replacement paper, unrelated orthopaedic study, unrelated study

**Gold snippet (first 300 chars):**
> Breast cancer is the most common malignancy afflicting women, and the most common cancer overall
> in Jordan. A woman's decision to go for screening is influenced by her social support network.
> This study aims to explore Jordanian men's individual and contextual perspectives on women's
> breast cancer screening decisions...

**Top-1 retrieved snippet (first 250 chars):**
> Unicompartmental replacement can be an alternative to tibial osteotomy in younger, active patients
> with unicompartmental knee disease. In unicompartmental replacement, the other compartments and knee
> ligaments are largely untouched...

**Why BM25 failed:** Figurative/non-literal query language.
The query is a rhetorical/cultural expression used to ask about men's attitudes toward breast cancer
screening. The words "smell", "rose", and "throw" are non-medical and appear in many unrelated
contexts. BM25 has no mechanism to understand that this is a figurative question about gender
attitudes in healthcare. There is zero lexical bridge between the query's metaphorical language and
the gold document's academic oncology vocabulary. This is a fundamental limitation of sparse
retrieval — it cannot handle semantics beyond surface token matching.

---

## Summary of Failure Patterns

| Pattern | Description | Mitigation |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| **Topic collision** | Generic procedural keywords (safe, efficacious) match unrelated studies | Dense retrieval (embeddings) or query expansion |
| **Figurative language** | Non-literal queries have no lexical bridge to gold documents | Semantic search via bi-encoders (e.g., MedBERT, BioSentBERT) |
| **Synonym/abbreviation gap** | Query uses full term; gold uses abbreviation (or vice versa) | Medical NER + synonym expansion |
| **Long-context dilution** | Evidence spread across multiple chunks; no single chunk exceeds the overlap threshold | Larger chunk size or passage fusion |
